summary:
The Dawenkou culture was a Neolithic culture located in the Shandong and Jiangsu provinces of eastern China, dating from approximately 4300 to 2600 BCE. Contemporary in its later phases with the [[event:36|Yangshao culture]] to its west, the Dawenkou culture developed a distinct regional tradition with characteristic pottery forms, burial practices, and evidence of significant social stratification. It represents an important strand in the cultural development of eastern China that eventually transitioned into the Longshan culture.

Dawenkou communities cultivated millet and rice and herded pigs and cattle. Their pottery tradition is notable for its thin-walled, elegant vessels including tall-stemmed cups, flasks, and beakers in fine white, gray, or black ware. A remarkable feature of Dawenkou burial practice is the high frequency of tooth extraction and dental modification — specifically the removal of upper lateral incisors — which served as a social marker distinguishing members of the community who had undergone this practice. Grave goods show substantial variation in wealth, with some individuals buried with large numbers of ceramic vessels, jade ornaments, and pig mandibles (a probable prestige good), while others had minimal accompaniments.

The Dawenkou culture provides some of the earliest evidence in China for pronounced wealth differentiation in burials, foreshadowing the hereditary elite hierarchies of the Bronze Age Chinese states. A number of Dawenkou pottery marks — incised symbols on vessels — have been tentatively interpreted as early evidence of proto-writing, though this remains speculative. The culture is ancestral to the Longshan culture, which would exhibit even greater social complexity and technological sophistication.
